docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
I'm Shervin Babaei, and this is my documentation site for ACIT 2811 (UX/UI).

This site is a UX redesign case study where I analyze and improve a real-world website with poor usability. Each week documents my process, from research and user analysis to wireframes and final design decisions.

docs.title: Who This Is For
docs.text:
This site is written for:

  • Instructors evaluating my work
  • Classmates following the same UX process

It may also be useful for anyone interested in:

  • UX design
  • Website usability improvements
  • Real-world design case studies

docs.title: Tone & Writing Approach
docs.text:
The goal is to write like I’m explaining my work to a colleague:

  • Clear
  • Direct
  • Reflective

I use a conversational tone with simple language so ideas are easy to follow.

Tone feedback (peer-reviewed):

  • Clear
  • Structured
  • Slightly conversational

docs.title: Writing Quality Checks
docs.text:
To improve clarity and readability, I reviewed my writing using:

  • Grammarly and AI (conciseness + grammar)
  • Manual editing (active voice + clarity)

docs.title: What You'll Find Here
docs.text:
This site documents my learning and practical work in:

  • UX design principles and usability
  • Design thinking process
  • User research (personas, stories, scenarios)
  • Information architecture and navigation
  • Wireframing and prototyping (Figma)
  • Real-world UX redesign decisions

docs.title: Weekly Reflections
docs.text:
Each week focuses on a specific part of the UX process.

You’ll see:

  • What I worked on
  • Why I made certain decisions
  • What I learned from the process

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• Home - Overview of the project
  • Weekly Reflections - Step-by-step UX process
  • Glossary - Definitions of UX terms used

docs.title: Course
docs.text: ACIT 2811 – UX/UI Term: Winter 2026

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
This page explains technical terms and design concepts used throughout this site. If you come across an unfamiliar word in any weekly reflection, you can find a plain-language definition here.

Crazy 8s: A fast-paced ideation exercise where you sketch eight different ideas in eight minutes. The goal is quantity over quality - forcing you to move past your first instinct and explore a wider range of solutions before committing to one.

Design Thinking: A problem-solving approach that starts with understanding people before proposing solutions. It follows five stages: Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, and Test. The key idea is to define the problem clearly before jumping to answers.

Figma: A browser-based design tool used to create wireframes, prototypes, and user interface layouts. It's widely used in UX and product design because it's easy to iterate on and share with collaborators.

Five Ws: A framework for defining a problem by answering five questions: Who is affected? What is the issue? Where does it occur? When does it happen? Why does it matter? In UX, it helps designers focus on user needs before jumping to solutions.

High-Fidelity Wireframe: A detailed, near-final mockup of a design that includes real typography, colors, and content. It closely resembles the finished product and is used to test visual decisions and refine the user experience before development.

Heuristic: A usability guideline used to evaluate whether a design is easy to use. The Nielsen Norman Group's 10 heuristics are the most widely used set - they cover things like consistency, error prevention, and minimalist design.

Information Architecture (IA): The practice of organizing and labeling content so users can find what they need. It covers navigation structure, page hierarchy, and the language used to describe categories - essentially, how a site is structured from the user's point of view.

Low-Fidelity Wireframe: A rough, simplified layout of a design - often in black and white with placeholder content. It focuses on structure and navigation rather than visuals, making it easy to test ideas quickly and cheaply before investing in detailed design work.

Persona: A fictional but research-based character that represents a type of user. Personas include goals, frustrations, and behaviors to help designers make decisions that serve real people rather than abstract assumptions.

UX (User Experience): How a person feels when using a product or website - covering ease of use, clarity, efficiency, and overall satisfaction. Good UX means users can complete their goals without frustration or confusion.

User Flow: A diagram showing the steps a user takes to complete a task on a website or app. It maps out every screen or decision point from start to finish, helping designers spot gaps or unnecessary friction in the experience.

User Scenario: A short narrative describing how a specific user might interact with a product in a real-world context. Scenarios help designers think through edge cases and ensure the design works for realistic situations, not just ideal ones.

User Story: A short, goal-focused statement written from a user's perspective, usually in the format: "As a [type of user], I want to [do something] so that [I achieve a goal]." User stories keep design decisions tied to real needs rather than features for their own sake.

docs.title: Communication & UX
docs.text: Summary: In this project, I analyze and redesign a website with poor UX. I selected Pacific Northwest X-Ray Inc. because of its cluttered layout, inconsistent styling, and overwhelming link structure. My goal is to document the full UX process independently while strengthening my analytical, design, and documentation skills. This work will serve as both a learning experience and a potential portfolio case study.

docs.title: Task Overview
docs.text: For this course, I identified a website with poor user experience (UX) and communication, then used it throughout the term to document my design and analysis process.

docs.title: Website Selection Process
docs.text: I began by reviewing X-ray–related websites with weak UX and communication. After narrowing down my options to three -Craigslist, the West Vancouver Recreation Center, and Pacific Northwest X-Ray Inc.-I evaluated each through discussion and analysis using ChatGPT. Ultimately, I selected Pacific Northwest X-Ray Inc. as my focus.

docs.title: Rationale for Selection
docs.text:
This website stood out for several UX issues. The design feels visually cluttered, with excessive colors and inconsistent styling that detract from the user experience.

The frequent use of yellow underlined links reduces emphasis on the most important content, making it difficult for users to prioritize information. Additionally, many links compete for attention across the interface. I believe condensing these into fewer, higher-priority options would significantly improve readability and user interaction.

Explore the Pacific Northwest X-Ray Inc. website

docs.title: Working Approach
docs.text: I'm working alone on this project to maintain focus and strengthen my critical and analytical thinking skills.

docs.title: Course Goals
docs.text: Through this class, I'm hoping to gain a comprehensive understanding of the UX documentation and design process, including hands-on work in Figma. I also want to improve my writing, critical thinking, and analytical skills. Looking ahead, I plan to redesign the website over the summer and either propose it to the owners or include it in my portfolio as a case study.

docs.title: Low-Fidelity Wireframe Testing
docs.text: Summary: This week I tested the low-fidelity wireframes built in Week ⅞ with real users in class. The goal was to observe how users navigate the designs and identify confusion before moving to high-fidelity work. The results were mostly positive, with only minor layout adjustments needed.

docs.title: How the Testing Worked
docs.text:
If you haven't seen the wireframes yet, check out Week ⅞ for context on what I built and why.

In small groups, each person presented their wireframes to a partner while that partner tried to complete basic tasks - finding a product, navigating to a specific section, and so on. As the designer, I watched without explaining anything. That silence matters: if you're narrating, you're compensating for a design problem.

docs.title: What I Observed
docs.text:
I paid attention to three things:

  • Where users hesitated - a pause usually means a label or layout choice isn't clear
  • Where they asked questions - questions reveal assumptions the design makes that users don't share
  • Where they moved quickly - that's confirmation the structure is working

docs.title: Feedback & Findings
docs.text:
Most users moved through my design without major confusion. The overall structure and flow held up well. A few small issues came up - minor labeling tweaks and one layout adjustment - but nothing that changed the core structure.

No major redesign is needed before moving to high-fidelity wireframes.

docs.title: What I Learned
docs.text: Watching someone use your design without coaching is uncomfortable, but it's the most honest feedback you can get. The small issues I found here are far cheaper to fix now than after investing time in high-fidelity work.

docs.title: High-Fidelity Wireframes
docs.text: Summary: This week I moved from structure to visual design, producing high-fidelity wireframes for the PNWX redesign. The visual decisions - typography, color, and layout - were each tied to a specific usability goal rather than personal preference. Three Nielsen Norman heuristics guided every major choice.

docs.title: Where This Fits in the Project
docs.text: If you're jumping in here, here's the quick context: I'm redesigning Pacific Northwest X-Ray Inc. - a medical imaging supplier with a cluttered, hard-to-navigate site. The low-fidelity wireframes (Week ⅞) defined the structure. This week, I gave that structure a visual language.

docs.title: The Core Problem
docs.text:
The original site suffers from visual noise - competing colors, inconsistent styling, and skull icons on navigation links. Every element fights for attention, so nothing stands out.

My redesign strips this back to essentials and makes every visual choice functional.

docs.title: Typography
docs.text: I paired Raleway (headers) with Inter (body text). Raleway gives the brand a distinct character; Inter is optimized for screen legibility at small sizes. Together they balance identity with readability.

docs.title: Color Palette
docs.text:
I moved to a three-color system with a clear job for each:

  • Deep Navy - establishes stability and structure
  • Light Blue - supports secondary layout elements without competing
  • Green - reserved exclusively for buttons and calls to action

Users always know where to click because green appears nowhere else.

docs.title: Heuristic #1 - Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
docs.text: Product cards show only what users need to make a decision. No decorative clutter competing with the product itself.

docs.title: Heuristic #4 - Consistency and Standards
docs.text: Navigation looks and behaves the same on every page. Users build a mental model once and it holds throughout the site.

docs.title: Heuristic #6 - Recognition Over Recall
docs.text: On-screen filters and sidebar categories let users find products by scanning - not by memorizing a site map.

docs.title: Wireframe Previews
docs.text:
Figure 1: Main product page

Figure 2: Individual product page

docs.title: What I Learned
docs.text: Anchoring visual decisions to heuristics kept me from making choices based on personal taste. Every time I picked a color or font, I had to ask: "What usability problem does this solve?" That constraint made the design more defensible - and better.

docs.title: Usability Principles & Market Research
docs.text: Summary: This week, I looked at core usability principles, design thinking, project planning, and market research to strengthen the foundation of the term project. I focused on usability, credibility, accessibility, and structured problem-solving through the five stages of design thinking. I reviewed competitor websites and defined a formal proposal, which helped align the project with real-world standards and identified opportunities for improvement.

docs.title: Seven Usability Factors
docs.text:
The seven usability factors play a key role in designing effective websites and products. They strongly influence how users interact with a system and perceive its quality.

The most important factors, in my view, are usable, findable, credible, and accessible, as they directly affect users' ability to complete tasks.

If a site isn't usable or content is hard to find, users become frustrated and leave. Credibility builds trust, especially when users share personal or payment information. Accessibility ensures inclusive and ethical design. Without these factors, even visually appealing websites can fail.

docs.title: Design Thinking
docs.text:
I used design thinking, a user-centered approach focused on understanding users before proposing solutions. Although the process can feel abstract at first, it helps prevent jumping to conclusions without fully defining the problem.

The design thinking process has five stages:

  • Empathize: Understand users through observation and feedback
  • Define: Clearly identify the core problem
  • Ideate: Generate multiple solution ideas
  • Prototype: Create simple versions of solutions
  • Test: Gather feedback to improve the design

docs.title: Term Project Proposal
docs.text:
We were required to create a proposal outlining our goals for the term project. Our instructor will evaluate progress against this proposal, reflecting real-world expectations of accountability and performance.

My proposal focused on redesigning the Pacific Northwest X-Ray website by simplifying navigation, improving content hierarchy, and reducing visual clutter.

View Term Project Proposal (PDF)

docs.title: Market Research
docs.text: As part of my market research, I reviewed existing websites related to my project to understand current design approaches and identify strengths and weaknesses.

docs.title: ProboMedical
docs.text:
Figure 1: ProboMedical homepage

ProboMedical presents a modern interface with large product imagery and a structured navigation system. The homepage uses clear sections to highlight services, products, and featured content.

However, some images appear inconsistent in quality and resolution, which slightly reduces the professional feel of the site. Additionally, some sections contain dense blocks of content that could be simplified for faster scanning.

Despite these issues, the site demonstrates a stronger hierarchy than the PNWX website and provides a clearer starting point for users exploring medical imaging products.

docs.title: GE Healthcare Shop
docs.text:
Figure 2: GE Healthcare Shop homepage

GE Healthcare’s store is more complex because it supports a large catalog of medical equipment and parts. While the site contains extensive product information, the layout feels somewhat inconsistent across sections.

Navigation menus and product pages sometimes use different visual structures, which can make the experience feel fragmented. However, the site performs well in terms of credibility, branding, and access to technical documentation.

Reviewing this site helped me understand how large healthcare companies organize extensive product catalogs and technical resources.

docs.title: User Research: Personas, Stories, and Scenarios
docs.text: Summary: This week, I focused on developing a deeper understanding of users through personas, user stories, and user scenarios. By defining realistic goals and task flows for different user types, the project clarified what the website must support and where friction currently exists. This process helped me reinforce a user-centered mindset that guides design decisions based on real needs rather than assumptions.

docs.title: Personas
docs.text:
I created two primary personas to represent the main users of the PNWX website.

Dr. Sarah Martinez represents experienced clinical decision-makers who need detailed product information, compliance documentation, and efficient reordering for multiple locations. She values accuracy, reliability, and time-saving tools.

Mike Chen represents non-technical practice managers who focus on speed, clarity, and budget control. He needs plain-language descriptions, transparent pricing, and a mobile-friendly experience.

Creating these personas helped me design for real people with different goals, rather than a generic "user."

docs.title: User Stories
docs.text:
User stories helped me translate each persona's needs into clear goals. Writing them in the "As a user, I want…" format made it easier to stay focused on outcomes instead of features.

For example:

  • Dr. Sarah needs downloadable spec sheets and product comparisons to support compliance and purchasing decisions.
  • Mike needs simple descriptions, visible pricing, and mobile ordering so he can work quickly without technical knowledge.

These stories clarified why certain features matter and helped prioritize what the redesigned website should support first.

docs.title: User Scenarios
docs.text:
User scenarios helped me walk through realistic tasks step by step, such as:

  • Downloading product specification sheets
  • Comparing similar products
  • Viewing transparent pricing and adding items to a cart

Mapping these flows showed where PNWX creates friction and where a redesigned experience could remove unnecessary steps. It also helped me think about navigation, labeling, and content placement more intentionally.

docs.title: What I Learned
docs.text:
This week reinforced how important it is to design from the user's perspective, not my own. Personas, stories, and scenarios gave structure to my thinking and made design decisions easier to justify.

Instead of asking "What should the website include?", I'm now asking:

  • "What is the user trying to do?"
  • "What information do they need right now?"
  • "What would slow them down or confuse them?"

This shift now guides every wireframe and design decision I make in the future.

docs.title: Problem Definition, Ideation, and Information Architecture
docs.text: Summary: This week, I focused on defining the core problem before beginning design. I emphasized user needs, goals, and clear information structure over visuals or features. Through the Five Ws, scope definition, ideation, and Information Architecture, the project established a logical, user-centered foundation for improving navigation and usability. Overall, this process reinforced a problem-first mindset that will guide future design and development decisions.

docs.title: Problem Statement & The Five Ws
docs.text:
Writing a problem statement helped me step back and focus on what problem the website is actually trying to solve. Using the Five Ws gave structure to my thinking and prevented vague assumptions.

  • Who: Identifying the users highlighted that different users have different goals, levels of experience, and time constraints.

  • What: Clarifying what the site offers helped define the main focus of the experience instead of trying to do everything at once.

  • Where: Recognizing that users access the site online emphasized the need for accessibility across devices.

  • When: Understanding that users often visit the site when they need information quickly reinforced the importance of efficiency.

  • Why: Defining the reason for the redesign made it clear that the goal is to reduce confusion and improve usability, not just to refresh the look.

docs.title: My website Five Ws
docs.text:
• Who: The primary users are medical professionals working in clinical or industry environments. They may have limited time and need fast, reliable access to products and information.

  • What: The website will provide a significantly improved navigation system, clear headings, and an organized structure. The goal is to help medical professionals quickly locate information and complete purchases efficiently.

  • Where: The platform will be accessed online, primarily on desktop and laptop devices. However, responsive design should still be considered to ensure accessibility across screen sizes.

  • When: Users will visit the website when they need critical information or products urgently. Therefore, speed, clarity, and direct access to key content are essential.

  • Why: The redesign aims to reduce confusion, improve usability, and ensure that medical professionals can access accurate information quickly. This supports better decision-making and minimizes the risk of delays or misinformation.

docs.title: Scope & Feature Definition
docs.text:
Defining the scope helped set realistic boundaries for the project. Listing what would and wouldn't be implemented ensured that the focus stayed on structure and clarity, rather than advanced functionality or visuals.

This step made it easier to prioritize essential work and avoid feature creep.

docs.title: Ideation Process
docs.text:
During ideation, I generated ideas without judgment. Techniques such as brainstorming, worst possible ideas, and Crazy 8s helped expand my thinking and explore a wide range of possibilities.

Brainstorming helped me consider multiple approaches to organizing content and navigation. This helped surface different ways users might move through the site and access information.

The worst possible idea technique was useful for identifying common usability mistakes. By intentionally thinking about bad design choices, it became easier to recognize what should be avoided in the final structure.

Crazy 8s pushed me to think quickly and sketch multiple wireframe layout ideas without overthinking details. This rapid process encouraged creativity and prevented me from fixating on a single solution too early.

Overall, this phase emphasized generating a high quantity of ideas rather than focusing on quality upfront, which made later decision-making more efficient and informed.

docs.title: Information Architecture (IA)
docs.text:
I used Information Architecture to tie everything together by focusing on how users find information.

  • Navigation hierarchy helped balance between deep and shallow structures.
  • I used labels that match user language rather than internal terminology.
  • Creating a content inventory and sitemap clarified how all pages relate to each other.
  • Wireframes helped visualize structure without being distracted by styling.

This step showed how organization directly affects usability.

docs.title: What I Learned
docs.text:
This week taught me the importance of problem-first thinking. By clearly defining users, goals, and structure early on, future design and development decisions become easier to justify and more user-focused.

Instead of asking "What features should this site have?", I'm now asking:

  • "What is the user trying to do?"
  • "What information do they need right now?"
  • "What would slow them down or confuse them?"

This mindset will guide my future wireframes, layouts, and overall design decisions.

docs.title: User Flow & Low-Fidelity Wireframes
docs.text: Summary: This week, I worked on two major deliverables: user flows and low-fidelity wireframes. I built on the personas and user stories from Week 3 by mapping out how each user type moves through the site, then translated those paths into wireframe layouts using Figma. I focused on structure and navigation - not visual styling.

docs.title: From User Stories to User Flows
docs.text:
If you haven't read Week 3 yet, that's where I developed the personas and user stories that drive this work. Each user flow here maps directly to one of those three user stories.

Personally, creating the flows felt manageable because I already had a clear picture of how users would move through the site. My goal was to make those mental models visible and testable.

docs.title: Why I Skipped Paper Wireframes
docs.text: Our instructor suggested starting on paper, but I found it hard to iterate quickly that way. Figma let me experiment with layouts faster and gave me a cleaner record to build on in future weeks.

docs.title: What the Wireframes Focus On
docs.text:
The layouts prioritize two things:

  • Simplified navigation - fewer clicks to reach key content
  • Improved content hierarchy - most important information appears first, so users can scan without scrolling far

Working at low fidelity kept me focused on structure and usability. I deliberately avoided styling decisions - those come later.

docs.title: What I Learned
docs.text: Translating user stories into actual screen layouts makes abstract goals concrete. When I asked "where does this user go next?", the answer often revealed gaps in the original site's structure that text analysis alone had missed.
